strands.title: Skill Category 1: Read and Comprehend Latin Poetry and Prose
Skill Category 2: Describing Style and Context of Latin Poetry and Prose
Skill Category 3: Analyze Latin Poetry and Prose
Construct interpretations of Latin texts' main ideas and meanings.
Recognize what Latin words and phrases mean in texts
Analyze how repetition functions as a stylistic device in Latin writing.
Identify and interpret main ideas and overall meaning in Latin texts.
Provide definitions for Latin words and phrases
Repetition stresses importance, enhances memorability, and creates rhythm or musicality.
Alliteration repeats initial consonants to emphasize ideas, enhance rhythm, and reinforce emotion.
Determine word meanings using surrounding contextual clues
Analyze the intended effect and rhetorical purpose of Latin texts.
Interpret authorial perspective, speaker stance, and character attitudes in Latin.
Anaphora repeats opening words across successive units to emphasize ideas and create momentum.
Explain grammatical features' role in Latin meaning and function
Explain how Latin nouns create and affect textual meaning
Support textual interpretations with targeted evidence and contextual analysis.
Parallel structure repeats grammatical forms to balance ideas, emphasize, and establish relationships.
Analyze how word order functions as a stylistic device in Latin writing.
Nouns show gender, number, case through endings organized in declensions
Provide direct Latin quotations that substantiate textual interpretations.
Nominative case identifies the verb's subject
Clarify connections between cited passages and interpretive claims.
Chiasmus inverts paired elements to create emphasis and direct reader attention.
Synchysis interlocks elements in a-b-a-b format to create unexpected emphatic moments.
Demonstrate how historical and cultural context reinforces textual interpretation.
Nominative case used for both ideas with linking verbs
Show how literary devices and style choices strengthen interpretive arguments.
Genitive case shows possession or belonging relationships
Enjambment delays final words to succeeding lines for suspense or emphasis.
Genitive shows description, part-to-whole, or action-related nouns
Analyze how similes and metaphors function as stylistic devices in Latin texts.
Dative case indicates indirect object or beneficiary of action
Similes make explicit comparisons using connective words to vivify descriptive imagery.
Dative case expresses purpose in double dative constructions
Metaphors make implicit comparisons using figurative language to create vivid imagery and connections.
Dative with sum/desum expresses possession or deprivation
Analyze the characteristics and features of meter in Latin poetry.
Latin meter uses predictable long-short syllable sequences; feet include dactyls, spondees, trochees.
Accusative case marks the direct object receiving action
Elision suppresses word endings before vowels or h to maintain metrical patterns.
Accusative case expresses duration of time
Ablative shows means, agent, manner, time, place, separation
Epic poetry uses dactylic hexameter with specific foot patterns in Vergilian tradition.
Ablative shows respect or viewpoint for statement validity
Analyze the defining characteristics and features of Latin literary genres.
Epistles are major Roman genre reflecting daily life; some are highly literary and revised.
Ablative with adjective provides descriptive noun modification
Epic poetry is stylized genre where poets use traditional elements while adding personal contributions.
Ablative with comparatives shows the standard of comparison
Ablative absolute shows time or circumstance of action
Epic poems open with proem and muse invocation, then begin plot in medias res.
Causa and gratia in ablative express purpose after genitive
Gods are always involved in epic narrative, sometimes driving the plot forward directly.
Locative shows location for cities; accusative/ablative for motion
Epic heroes experience divine help and hindrance; often descend to underworld for quests.
Latin literature includes epigrams, historiography, love poems, didactic, drama, dialogue, oratory, novels.
Vocative case identifies the person or object being addressed
Explain how Latin verbs and verbals create and affect textual meaning
Analyze references and allusions within Latin texts and their contexts.
Verb person, number, tense, voice, mood shown through conjugations
Indicative mood expresses factual or actual reality statements
